test_design: M01-D blind verification — AI's verse picks per finding

results.rationale: God produces dismay in rebels (Psa 2:5, 90:7, Jer 49:37) and commands against dismay in his people (Jer 1:17, 30:10, 46:27); God's hidden face dismays (Psa 30:7, 104:29).
Mal 2:5 shows fear-of-God as the creature's covenantal disposition toward the divine, not a characteristic God possesses—supporting the asymmetry finding.

The commanded-against-dismay corpus (Jer 1:17; 51:7; 23:4; 30:10; 46:27) shows dismay can be resisted when the divine word speaks, revealing its created purpose as honest threat-registration checked by divine commission.
Dismay contexts are consistently fallen-condition realities—divine judgment, catastrophe, hiding of God's face—with no evidence of dismay as an original creation design feature.
Jer 30:10 and 46:27 promise salvation and quiet rest as the antidote to dismay; Jer 23:4 promises flock will no longer be dismayed—pointing to eschatological undismayed composure.
Mal 2:5 is the corpus's nearest point to God-and-fear, yet it describes the priest's fear before God, not God's own fear—confirming fear as exclusively creaturely.
Psa 6:3 (soul greatly dismayed), Eze 7:27 (hands paralyzed), Job 32:15 (speechlessness), Job 23:16 (heart faint) together evidence dismay as sudden inner collapse of coherence and capacity.
The primary terms ba.hal, cha.tat, ze.va.ah, re.tet, tim.ma.hon all appear in corpus; ba.hal's dual shock-and-rash-urgency character is evidenced in Ecc 7:9, 8:3 vs. Job/Psa dismay contexts.
Soul greatly dismayed (Psa 6:3), heart made faint (Job 23:16), hands paralyzed (Eze 7:27), limbs giving way (Dan 5:6) all confirm the constitutional inner-dissolution orientation of the term.

Job 4:5 (dismay overtaking), Job 22:10 (sudden terror overwhelming), Psa 30:7 (God hiding face → dismayed), Psa 104:29 (face hidden → dismayed), Dan 5:6 (thoughts alarming, limbs giving way) all show dismay as a condition happening to the person.
Dismay's structure is simple: external source → inner collapse (Job 22:10; Dan 5:6). The constituent elements are source and recipient rather than multiple internal sub-components.

Psa 6:3 (soul as seat), Job 23:16 (heart faint), Dan 5:6 (thoughts → color change/limb failure), Dan 5:10 (queen identifies thoughts as dismay's origin), Dan 7:28 (thoughts alarmed, color changed, kept in heart).
Jer 1:17 commands courage as opposite of dismay; Job 39:22 defines the war horse's courage as complete absence of dismay—establishing composure/courage as the structural opposite.
Jer 1:17; 51:7; 10:2; 17:18; 30:10; 46:27 explicitly forbid dismay, showing the commanded-against corpus excludes prophetic and pastoral capitulation to inner collapse before opponents.

Dismay texts show dissolution of capacity to function (Isa 13:8 overwhelmed like birth pangs; Dan 5:6 limbs give way; Job 32:15 complete speechlessness), distinguishing dismay from acute fear that may preserve responsive capacity.
2Sa 4:1 (news collapse), Psa 48:5 (sight of God's city → flight), Psa 2:5 (divine judicial), Dan 5:6 (bodily collapse), Ecc 5:2; 8:3; 7:9 (rash agitation) evidence the four operational modes.
Ecc 7:9; 8:3; 5:2 (rash-agitation form); Job 32:15; Eze 7:27 (paralysis-collapse form); Psa 2:5; Jer 49:37 (divine-judicial imposed form) show contextual variation.

Job 32:15 (friends dismayed into complete speechlessness); Job 23:15 (standing before God produces overpowering upheaval); Isa 21:3 (dismay so overwhelming prophet cannot see) show speech-collapse mode.
Job 32:15 (not a word to say), Isa 21:3 (cannot hear, cannot see), Dan 5:6 (limbs give way, knees knock) show cognitive and verbal faculties failing first.
Speechlessness (Job 32:15; Isa 21:3) versus violent agitation (Dan 11:44 alarming news converting to furious rage) demonstrate the variation in immediate response within ba.hal.
Psa 6:3 (soul in turmoil—affective); Job 23:16 (heart faint—affective); Dan 5:6 (thoughts alarmed—cognitive; limbs give way—somatic); Dan 7:28 (thoughts alarmed—cognitive; color changed—somatic).
Dan 5:6 (thoughts alarm → color change, limb-failure, knees knocking) and Dan 7:28 (thoughts alarmed → color changed) both show inner-cognitive → somatic-expressive movement.

Dan 4:19 (understanding terrible message → speech overridden), Dan 5:6 (thoughts → color/limbs), Dan 7:28 (thoughts → color → kept in heart), Job 32:15 (dismayed → speechless) show event→inner disruption→somatic pattern.
Psa 6:3 explicitly states 'my soul also is greatly troubled'—the meaning summary identifies the soul (nefesh) as the explicit seat of the profound turmoil.
Psa 6:3's soul-level location of dismay reveals inner collapse reaching to the deepest experiential self, penetrating to the core of personal existence, not merely surface emotion.
Job 23:16 (God made Job's heart faint—heart as constitutional seat), Dan 5:6 (inner person struck—heart-seat), Dan 5:10 (dismay originates in thought/heart), Dan 7:28 (kept in heart).
Job 23:16 (heart faint—feeling/sustaining function disrupted); Dan 5:10 (dismay originating in thought—knowing function disrupted) show both knowing and feeling functions engaged.
Dan 4:19 (thoughts alarmed—mind as locus), Dan 5:10 (dismay originates in inner life of thought), Dan 7:28 (thoughts greatly alarmed) all locate dismay significantly in the mind.

Dan 4:19 (alarmed thoughts overriding speech), Dan 5:10 (thoughts as originating site), Dan 7:28 (thoughts alarmed before color changes) show thought-function as primary mind engagement, with comprehension producing collapse.
Psa 6:3 ('my soul also is greatly troubled') is the clearest corpus instance of a soul-level location distinct from both heart and mind for dismay.
Psa 6:3's nefesh-location reveals dismay penetrating to the personal-life center; the soul as seat of experienced aliveness is disrupted, making this constitutionally deeper than heart-disturbance.
Dan 5:6 (limbs give way, knees knock); Isa 21:3 (cannot hear, cannot see—eyes/ears affected); Job 32:15 (speechlessness—speech faculties) name multiple body-parts as sites of dismay's manifestation.
Dan 5:6 (color change, limb-failure), Isa 21:3 (cannot see), Job 32:15 (speechlessness) are expressive of complete inner collapse and indicative of severity—bodily function overwhelmed.
Dan 5:6 and 7:28 show external stimulus → inner alarm → somatic expression (color, limbs); Isa 21:3 shows vision → inner dismay → perceptive shutdown, all primarily inner-person to body direction.

Dan 5:6 and 7:28 confirm dismay is an inner event producing somatic consequences (soul-to-body direction); the persistence pattern in Dan 7:28 (kept in heart) hints at potential feedback loop deepening the state.

2Sa 4:1 (Abner's death = external news), Psa 30:7 (God hiding face = external action), Psa 104:29 (face hidden), Isa 13:8 (divine judgment), Dan 11:44 (alarming news), Dan 5:6 (writing on wall) all show external origin.
Isa 21:3 (dismay so overwhelming prophet cannot see—sight distorted/shut down); Job 32:15 (friends dismayed into speechlessness—verbal-perceptive loop shut down) show sight and speech engagement.
Isa 21:3 (cannot see), Job 32:15 (speechlessness), Dan 5:6 (color change and limb-failure as perceptive-system overwhelm) all show dismay impairing the perceptive faculty.

Eze 26:18 (coastlands dismayed at Tyre's passing—communal relational rupture from witnessing collapse); Psa 83:15 (enemies to be terrified—dismay as relational/social weapon) show dismay impairing relational capacity.

Isa 21:3 (cognitive-perceptive shutdown: cannot see); Job 32:15 (rational and verbal faculties entirely shut down); Dan 7:28 (thoughts alarmed but Daniel resolves to keep matter inward—last cognitive act before color changes).
Isa 21:3 (perception shut down), Job 32:15 (speech/reason shut down), Dan 4:19 (thoughts racing—cognitive origin), Dan 7:28 (thoughts alarmed → color changes) all show dismay impairing cognition.
Isa 21:3 (cannot see—cognitive shutdown impairing memory retrieval); Job 21:6 (merely remembering dismays—memory triggering dismay, showing the bidirectional impairment) support this finding.
Psa 6:3 (soul greatly dismayed—profound turmoil driving anguished cry to God); Isa 13:8 (pangs, agony, anguish like birth-pangs); Job 21:6 (shuddering seizes flesh) show affective faculty engagement.
Dan 5:6 (inner terror so violent all other affect displaced—color change, limbs give way); Isa 13:8 (dismay monopolising inner life with anguish like birth pangs) show dismay overwhelming affect.
Isa 21:3 (cannot see—will to perceive collapsed); Eze 7:27 (hands paralyzed—will to act physically disabled); Job 32:15 (cannot answer—will to speak shut down) all show volitional dissolution.
Eze 7:27 (hands paralyzed—volitional capacity physically disabled); Job 32:15 (will to speak dissolved—friends wordless) are the strongest evidence of dismay impairing and dissolving volition.
Eze 7:27 (hands paralyzed—agency physically disabled); Psa 2:5 (God's wrathful speech producing dismay in kings—agency impaired by divine judicial action) evidence agency impairment.

Eze 7:27 (hands paralyzed—agency impaired); Psa 2:5 (terror bypassing deliberative agency); Jer 49:37 (God terrorising Elam—divine terror producing immediate inner breakdown) support agency bypass and impairment.

Psa 2:5 (God terrifying rebel kings), Jer 49:37 (God terrifying Elam), Psa 90:7 (wrath dismaying), Isa 30:31 (Lord's voice terror-striking Assyria), Deu 28:28 (confusion as divine curse), Zec 12:4 (God striking with panic).

Psa 2:5 (God dismays kings rebelling against him—conditional on defiance), Jer 49:37 (God terrifying Elam—conditional on their opposition), Isa 30:31 (Assyrian threat—conditional), Psa 90:7 (wrath as response to human finitude/sin) show conditional-responsive basis.

Psa 2:5 (terror against rebels), Jer 49:37 (fierce anger dismaying enemies), Isa 30:31 (Lord's rod), Deu 28:28 (divine curse), Zec 12:4 (protecting Judah) all reveal sovereignly just governance of inner composure.

Psa 6:3 (soul dismayed driving anguished cry 'how long'—dismay triggering prayer toward God); Psa 30:7 (dismay at God's hidden face producing prayer) show limited human-to-God movement through crisis-prayer.
Jer 1:17 (commanded not to let dismay grip—transformation to sustained composure grounded in divine commission); Jer 23:4; 30:10; 46:27 (promise of no-more-dismay as transformed stable condition).
Job 4:5 (suffering touching Job personally breaks inner composure); Psa 6:3 (soul greatly dismayed); Psa 6:2 (bones troubled); Job 23:15 (suffering before God producing dismay).
Job 4:5; 21:6; 22:10; 23:15; 23:16 show suffering directly producing dismay in Job—adversity is the consistent producer of the disordered inner collapse.
Jer 30:10 and 46:27 (salvation and quiet rest as antidote to dismay—eschatological no-more-dismay); Jer 23:4 (flock will no longer fear or be dismayed under faithful shepherds).
Psa 6:10; 83:17; Jer 8:9 (dismay and shame co-paired); Job 21:6 (dismay and shuddering together); Dan 5:6 (color change, limb-failure as somatic co-occurrence) show primary co-occurrences.

Dan 5:6 (writing → dismay → color change/limb failure); Dan 7:28 (visions → thoughts alarmed → color changed → kept in heart); Dan 4:19 (terrible message understood → dismay → speech overridden) demonstrate the sequence.
Dan 7:28 ('dismay works through Daniel's thoughts and changes his color'—thought-disruption as causal intermediary) and Dan 5:6 (thoughts alarming → somatic expression) show causal relationship.
Dan 5:6 (dismay → color change, limbs give way); Job 32:15 (dismayed → speechlessness); Eze 7:27 (dismay → hands paralyzed) show dismay producing bodily collapse, speechlessness, and paralysis.
H2729 cha.rad and H2731 cha.ra.dah (Judg 8:12; 1Sa 14:15 x2) and H0926 ba.hal (Judg 20:41) show root-level architecture generating multiple terms across adjacent characteristics in the corpus.

Job 21:6 (shuddering seizes flesh alongside dismay—trembling and dismay co-occurring); 1Sa 14:15 (trembling/panic = somatic expression while function partially intact vs. Dan 5:6 collapse) distinguish the two.

1Sa 14:15 (trembling throughout camp—body registering fear while military context still active) versus Dan 5:6 (limbs giving way, knees knocking—function dissolved) marks the boundary between trembling and dismay.

1Sa 14:15 (trembling as somatic expression while cohesion partially intact—degree); Job 21:6 (dismay + shuddering—kind: inner dissolution vs. somatic expression) show both degree and kind distinction.
Psa 6:3 (ba.hal), Judg 20:41 (ba.hal), Jer 1:17 (cha.tat), Deu 28:28 (tim.ma.hon), Zec 12:4 (tim.ma.hon), Jer 49:24 (re.tet), Isa 28:19 (ze.va.ah) together present the full term range.

Psa 6:3 (ba.hal—Niphal: dismay happening to the person, not chosen); Jer 1:17 (cha.tat—Niphal: be dismayed/be shattered); Job 32:15 (cha.tat—passive: they are dismayed) confirm passive/intensive grammatical range.
Dan 5:6: 'his thoughts alarmed him' (be.hal)—the term functions as the verbal expression of the inner event from which color change, limb-failure, and knocking knees all flow causally.
Psa 6:2-3 (psalm lament); Job 4:5; 21:6; 22:10; 23:15-16 (wisdom/dialogue); Dan 4:19; 5:6; 5:9; 5:10; 7:15; 7:28 (Aramaic apocalyptic narrative) cover the three primary literary forms.
Dan 5:6 ('the king's color changed, and his thoughts alarmed him; his limbs gave way, and his knees knocked together') most fully displays the inner-to-outer cascade of dismay in one verse.

Dan 5:6 (color change, limb-failure, knees knocking), Dan 5:9 (color changes), Dan 7:28 (color changed), Job 32:15 (complete speechlessness), Isa 21:3 (cannot hear/see), Eze 7:27 (hands paralyzed) evidence freeze-response pattern.

Dan 5:6 (color change, limbs give way, knees knock), Dan 5:9 (color changes), Job 32:15 (speechlessness), Isa 21:3 (cannot hear/see), Eze 7:27 (hands paralyzed) map onto dorsal vagal shutdown response.

Dan 7:28 (Daniel keeps matter in heart after color changes—partial address of inner retention during collapse); Dan 5:10 (queen urging inner mastery over dismay) partially address but do not fully cover the dissociative dimension.
